steps.title: ADS Component Identification
steps.objective: Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.
keyIdeas: The list of components including CPC, bonding conductors, earthing conductor, and protective devices.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installing or testing them.

Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single piece of equipment, but a coordinated safety system designed to prevent electric shock. For ADS to work, it requires two main things: a low-resistance path for fault current to flow, and a protective device to sense that current and cut the power.

The system is made up of several specific components. These include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) which runs to each individual outlet, and the Earthing Conductor which connects the whole building to the ground. It also includes Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding, which connect metal pipes to the earth terminal. Finally, the system relies on Protective Devices, such as fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), to perform the actual 'disconnection' when a fault occurs. In some installations, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the soil) is also required to provide the connection to the earth itself.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single piece of equipment, but a coordinated safety system designed to prevent electric shock. These include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) which runs to each individual outlet, and the Earthing Conductor which connects the whole building to the ground. It also includes Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding, which connect metal pipes to the earth terminal. Finally, the system relies on Protective Devices, such as fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), to perform the actual 'disconnection' when a fault occurs. In some installations, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the soil) is also required to provide the connection to the earth itself.

steps.title: Bonding and Protective Devices
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices.
keyIdeas: The specific roles of bonding conductors versus the role of circuit breakers or fuses in ADS.
Incorrectly identifying bonding types leads to non-compliance with BS 7671 and safety risks.

In an ADS system, different components have very different jobs. Protective devices, such as fuses and circuit breakers, are the 'switches' of the system. Their only job is to detect a high current during a fault and physically break the circuit to stop the flow of electricity.

Bonding conductors do not break the circuit; instead, they ensure safety by equalizing voltage. Main Protective Bonding connects incoming metal services, like gas and water mains, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs, these pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth, preventing a person from getting a shock by touching a pipe and an appliance at the same time. Supplementary Bonding is used in specific high-risk areas, like bathrooms, to connect metal parts together locally. While a fuse 'trips' to stop current, bonding 'links' metal parts to keep them at a safe, equal voltage.
Explanation B
anchorFacts: In an ADS system, different components have very different jobs. Protective devices, such as fuses and circuit breakers, are the 'switches' of the system. Their only job is to detect a high current during a fault and physically break the circuit to stop the flow of electricity.

This ensures that if a fault occurs, these pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth, preventing a person from getting a shock by touching a pipe and an appliance at the same time. Supplementary Bonding is used in specific high-risk areas, like bathrooms, to connect metal parts together locally. While a fuse 'trips' to stop current, bonding 'links' metal parts to keep them at a safe, equal voltage.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: The TT arrangement where the consumer provides their own connection to Earth via an electrode.
TT systems rely on the soil for the fault path, making the electrode critical for safety.

Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (Terre is French for Earth). The first 'T' tells us that the source of the electricity (the transformer) is connected directly to Earth. The second 'T' tells us that the consumer's installation is also connected to Earth through its own local connection.

In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-clad steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the building. Because the fault current must travel through the actual ground (soil) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is often higher than in other systems. This makes the local electrode the most critical component for safety in a TT installation, as it is the only path for fault current to reach the ground.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
keyIdeas: The difference between separate (S) and combined (C) neutral and earth conductors in the supply.
The type of TN system dictates how the MET is connected to the supplier's equipment.

TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the exposed metal parts are connected to the supplier's Neutral conductor. The final letter describes how the Earth and Neutral are arranged.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer's property. Usually, the supplier's cable sheath acts as the separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate.' In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into one single conductor (often called PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing). However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety.

However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the means of earthing; the CPC connects exposed conductive parts to the MET.
Using the wrong term during inspection and testing results in incorrect documentation and confusion.

While both are green and yellow wires, the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) and the Earthing Conductor have different roles based on their location in the system. A CPC is the safety wire for an individual circuit. For example, the green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a light switch or a socket is a CPC. Its job is to connect the metal casing of that specific equipment back to the consumer unit.

The Earthing Conductor is the 'master' earth wire for the entire installation. There is only one main Earthing Conductor in a standard building. It connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit to the actual means of earthing, such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod. If the Earthing Conductor is disconnected, the entire building loses its safety path. If a CPC is disconnected, only that one specific circuit loses its protection.
Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.

It connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit to the actual means of earthing, such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod. If the Earthing Conductor is disconnected, the entire building loses its safety path. If a CPC is disconnected, only that one specific circuit loses its protection.

The CPC is for one circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and ADS Components
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a typical domestic installation scenario.
keyIdeas: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a typical domestic installation scenario.
Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.

Step 4: Identify the Bonding Conductors. You notice two 10mm² green and yellow cables leaving the MET. One goes to the gas meter and one to the water stopcock. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
steps: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).
Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.

Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.

Identify the Bonding Conductors. You notice two 10mm² green and yellow cables leaving the MET. One goes to the gas meter and one to the water stopcock. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors.

Identify the Protective Devices. Inside the consumer unit, you see a row of 6A and 32A Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs). These are the protective devices that will trip to provide the 'Disconnection' part of ADS.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Always trace the path from the supply to the circuit. The Earthing Conductor is the main link to the source, while CPCs are the safety wires for individual circuits. If the earth and neutral are joined at the cutout, it's TN-C-S.

steps.hint: Look closely at the supplier's cutout; if the earth wire originates from the neutral side of the block, you are looking at a TN-C-S (PME) system.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A property uses a local earth electrode and does not rely on the supply company to provide the earth path.
steps: Identify the earthing system type from the scenario.

steps.questionText: Which earthing system is described in this scenario?
steps.hint: A local earth electrode points to TT.

steps.title: Practice: Identifying TN-C-S
steps.objective: Identify TN-C-S from the combined-and-separated supply arrangement.
keyIdeas: Verify the learner's ability to correctly name earthing systems and ADS components without assistance.
Understanding Check A
Scenario: You are working on a remote farm building.
Observation 1: The supplier has provided Phase and Neutral only; there is no earth terminal at the cutout.
Observation 2: You see a copper-clad rod driven into the soil outside the building.
Observation 3: A green and yellow conductor connects this rod back to the installation's Main Earthing Terminal.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are working on a remote farm building.
Observation 1: The supplier has provided Phase and Neutral only; there is no earth terminal at the cutout.
Observation 2: You see a copper-clad rod driven into the soil outside the building.
Observation 3: A green and yellow conductor connects this rod back to the installation's Main Earthing Terminal.
taskConstraints: Teach the meaning of the letter codes (T, N, S, C) before testing system identification.
Ensure the distinction between Earthing Conductor and CPC is explicitly clarified before the final practice.
Do not use terms like 'Ze' or 'Zs' as they are reserved for later lessons.
Do not drift into: Exposed conductive parts (e.g., steel conduit identification)
Do not drift into: Extraneous conductive parts (e.g., gas/water pipe identification)
Verify the learner's ability to correctly name earthing systems and ADS components without assistance.
steps.questionIntent: Identify a TT earthing system and its primary electrode component.

taskSkeleton.scenario: The supply neutral and earth are combined in one conductor outside the installation and separated at the service position.
steps: Identify the earthing system from the supply arrangement.

steps.questionText: Which earthing system is described in this scenario?
steps.hint: Combined first, then separated, points to TN-C-S.

steps.title: Integrative Close: Why TT Uses an RCD
steps.objective: Explain why TT systems usually need an RCD.
keyIdeas: Evaluate the learner's ability to select the correct earthing system type and associated components based on a specific supply description.
Integrative
The earthing system tells you how the installation connects back to the source or ground.
ADS also requires the correct named conductors and devices so faults are cleared safely.
Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
anchorFacts: The earthing system tells you how the installation connects back to the source or ground.
ADS also requires the correct named conductors and devices so faults are cleared safely.

Evaluate the learner's ability to select the correct earthing system type and associated components based on a specific supply description.
steps.questionIntent: Evaluate the learner's ability to select the correct earthing system type and associated components based on a specific supply description.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A TT installation depends on a local earth electrode and the earth path is relatively high resistance.
steps: Explain why this makes an RCD necessary.

steps.questionText: Using one short explanation, explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.
steps.hint: The key issue is not the label TT by itself; it is the resistance of the earth return path.

Grounded source text:
Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Grounded source excerpts:

Excerpt 1 (d9a6caef-6a5f-4977-9780-209ce55a5692): ## LO4 — Requirements for earthing systems **AC** 4.1 Identify earthing system types 4.2 Identify ADS component parts 4.3 Identify exposed conductive parts 4.4 Identify extraneous conductive parts 4.5 Identify components of an earth loop impedance path **Range** - Earthing systems: TT; TN-S; TN-C-S - ADS components: CPC; main protective bonding; supplementary bonding; earthing conductor; protective devices; earth electrode - Exposed conductive parts: steel conduit/trunking/tray; steel enclosures; metal accessories; metallic equipment - Extraneous conductive parts: metallic gas/oil/water pipes; steel ductwork; structural steel - Earth loop impedance path components: Zs; Ze; R1; R2; MET; supplier earth return path ---

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: A safety strategy where a fault causes a protective device to automatically cut off power to prevent electric shock.
terms.anchor: The coordinated system of earthing and fuses working together to kill the power during a fault.
terms.misconception: Thinking ADS is a single component like a circuit breaker, rather than a system of parts working together.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the consumer provides their own connection to Earth via a local electrode.
terms.anchor: Terre-Terre: Earth at the source and a separate Earth rod at the property.
terms.misconception: Believing the supplier provides the earth connection in a TT system.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the neutral and earth conductors are separate throughout the entire system.
terms.anchor: Terre-Neutral-Separate: A dedicated earth path provided by the supplier's cable sheath.
terms.misconception: Confusing the separate earth conductor with the combined neutral/earth used in TN-C-S.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated inside the installation.
terms.anchor: Terre-Neutral-Combined-Separate: Often called PME, where the supplier uses one wire for both neutral and earth.
terms.misconception: Thinking the earth and neutral remain combined once they enter the consumer's fuse board.

terms.simpleDefinition: The specific conductor that connects the metal parts of a circuit to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: The individual safety wire for a specific circuit, like the green/yellow wire in a twin and earth cable.
terms.misconception: Using the term CPC to describe the main wire that connects the whole house to the ground.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main conductor connecting the installation's Main Earthing Terminal to the means of earthing.
terms.anchor: The master link between the building's earth bar and the supplier's earth or the earth rod.
terms.misconception: Calling every green and yellow wire an 'Earthing Conductor' when most are actually CPCs or bonding.

terms.simpleDefinition: A conductor used to connect metal services like gas and water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: The wire that ensures metal pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth.
terms.misconception: Thinking these conductors are meant to carry fault current from a broken appliance.

terms.simpleDefinition: A metal rod, plate, or mesh driven into the ground to provide a physical connection to Earth.
terms.anchor: The copper-clad rod in the soil used primarily in TT systems.
terms.misconception: Assuming every installation requires a rod in the ground; TN systems usually use the supplier's cable.

terms.simpleDefinition: A component such as a fuse or circuit breaker designed to interrupt the flow of electricity during a fault.
terms.anchor: The 'switch' that trips or the fuse that blows to achieve the 'Disconnection' in ADS.
terms.misconception: Thinking the earth wire itself stops the electricity; the wire only provides the path that allows the device to trip.

anchorPhrases: Terre means Earth, Neutral means Neutral.
S is for Separate, C is for Combined.
The CPC protects the circuit; the Earthing Conductor protects the whole installation.
ADS requires a path (earthing) and a switch (protective device).
misconceptionTargets: Interchanging the terms CPC and Earthing Conductor.
Assuming TT systems use the supplier's cable for earthing.
Believing bonding conductors are the same as circuit protective conductors.
Thinking ADS is just a fancy name for a fuse.

teachChecks.title: ADS Component Identification
teachChecks.objective: Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.
retrievalTextLines: Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single piece of equipment, but a coordinated safety system designed to prevent electric shock. For ADS to work, it requires two main things: a low-resistance path for fault current to flow, and a protective device to sense that current and cut the power.

The system is made up of several specific components. These include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) which runs to each individual outlet, and the Earthing Conductor which connects the whole building to the ground. It also includes Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding, which connect metal pipes to the earth terminal. Finally, the system relies on Protective Devices, such as fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), to perform the actual 'disconnection' when a fault occurs. In some installations, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the soil) is also required to provide the connection to the earth itself.
teachChecks.hint: Think of ADS as a team of parts working together to provide a path and a switch.

teachChecks.title: Bonding and Protective Devices
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices.
retrievalTextLines: In an ADS system, different components have very different jobs. Protective devices, such as fuses and circuit breakers, are the 'switches' of the system. Their only job is to detect a high current during a fault and physically break the circuit to stop the flow of electricity.

Bonding conductors do not break the circuit; instead, they ensure safety by equalizing voltage. Main Protective Bonding connects incoming metal services, like gas and water mains, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs, these pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth, preventing a person from getting a shock by touching a pipe and an appliance at the same time. Supplementary Bonding is used in specific high-risk areas, like bathrooms, to connect metal parts together locally. While a fuse 'trips' to stop current, bonding 'links' metal parts to keep them at a safe, equal voltage.
teachChecks.hint: Protective devices stop the current; bonding conductors keep metal parts at the same voltage.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems
teachChecks.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
retrievalTextLines: Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (Terre is French for Earth). The first 'T' tells us that the source of the electricity (the transformer) is connected directly to Earth. The second 'T' tells us that the consumer's installation is also connected to Earth through its own local connection.

In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-clad steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the building. Because the fault current must travel through the actual ground (soil) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is often higher than in other systems. This makes the local electrode the most critical component for safety in a TT installation, as it is the only path for fault current to reach the ground.
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), and the consumer must provide their own rod.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
retrievalTextLines: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the exposed metal parts are connected to the supplier's Neutral conductor. The final letter describes how the Earth and Neutral are arranged.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer's property. Usually, the supplier's cable sheath acts as the separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate.' In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into one single conductor (often called PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing). However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety.
teachChecks.hint: S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Earth and Neutral share a wire in the street.

teachChecks.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
teachChecks.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
retrievalTextLines: While both are green and yellow wires, the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) and the Earthing Conductor have different roles based on their location in the system. A CPC is the safety wire for an individual circuit. For example, the green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a light switch or a socket is a CPC. Its job is to connect the metal casing of that specific equipment back to the consumer unit.

The Earthing Conductor is the 'master' earth wire for the entire installation. There is only one main Earthing Conductor in a standard building. It connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit to the actual means of earthing, such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod. If the Earthing Conductor is disconnected, the entire building loses its safety path. If a CPC is disconnected, only that one specific circuit loses its protection.
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is for one circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house.

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "ADS Component Identification",
      "objective": "Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single piece of equipment, but a coordinated safety system designed to prevent electric shock. For ADS to work, it requires two main things: a low-resistance path for fault current to flow, and a protective device to sense that current and cut the power.",
        "The system is made up of several specific components. These include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) which runs to each individual outlet, and the Earthing Conductor which connects the whole building to the ground. It also includes Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding, which connect metal pipes to the earth terminal. Finally, the system relies on Protective Devices, such as fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), to perform the actual 'disconnection' when a fault occurs. In some installations, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the soil) is also required to provide the connection to the earth itself."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team of parts working together to provide a path and a switch."
    },
    {
      "title": "Bonding and Protective Devices",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In an ADS system, different components have very different jobs. Protective devices, such as fuses and circuit breakers, are the 'switches' of the system. Their only job is to detect a high current during a fault and physically break the circuit to stop the flow of electricity.",
        "Bonding conductors do not break the circuit; instead, they ensure safety by equalizing voltage. Main Protective Bonding connects incoming metal services, like gas and water mains, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs, these pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth, preventing a person from getting a shock by touching a pipe and an appliance at the same time. Supplementary Bonding is used in specific high-risk areas, like bathrooms, to connect metal parts together locally. While a fuse 'trips' to stop current, bonding 'links' metal parts to keep them at a safe, equal voltage."
      ],
      "hint": "Protective devices stop the current; bonding conductors keep metal parts at the same voltage."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (Terre is French for Earth). The first 'T' tells us that the source of the electricity (the transformer) is connected directly to Earth. The second 'T' tells us that the consumer's installation is also connected to Earth through its own local connection.",
        "In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-clad steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the building. Because the fault current must travel through the actual ground (soil) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is often higher than in other systems. This makes the local electrode the most critical component for safety in a TT installation, as it is the only path for fault current to reach the ground."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), and the consumer must provide their own rod."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the exposed metal parts are connected to the supplier's Neutral conductor. The final letter describes how the Earth and Neutral are arranged.",
        "In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer's property. Usually, the supplier's cable sheath acts as the separate earth path.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate.' In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into one single conductor (often called PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing). However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety."
      ],
      "hint": "S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Earth and Neutral share a wire in the street."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "While both are green and yellow wires, the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) and the Earthing Conductor have different roles based on their location in the system. A CPC is the safety wire for an individual circuit. For example, the green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a light switch or a socket is a CPC. Its job is to connect the metal casing of that specific equipment back to the consumer unit.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the 'master' earth wire for the entire installation. There is only one main Earthing Conductor in a standard building. It connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit to the actual means of earthing, such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod. If the Earthing Conductor is disconnected, the entire building loses its safety path. If a CPC is disconnected, only that one specific circuit loses its protection."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is for one circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house."
    }
  ]
}

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact only.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- The deeper question must connect the explanation facts and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.

teachChecks.title: ADS Component Identification

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for in electrical safety?
Which specific component in an ADS system is responsible for physically breaking the circuit during a fault?
What is the name of the safety wire that runs to each individual outlet or light switch?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is a low-resistance path just as essential as a circuit breaker for the ADS system to function correctly?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The path allows fault current to flow back to the source
High current is needed to trigger the protective device
Without a path the device will not detect the fault
It ensures the disconnection happens fast enough to prevent shock
teachChecks.hint: Think of ADS as a team of parts working together to provide a path and a switch.

teachChecks.title: Bonding and Protective Devices

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary job of a protective device like a fuse when it detects a fault?
Which conductor is used to connect incoming metal services, such as gas and water mains, to the Main Earthing Terminal?
In which specific high-risk area of a building is supplementary bonding typically installed?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does bonding protect a person from electric shock even though it does not 'trip' like a circuit breaker?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It equalizes the voltage between metal parts
It keeps metal pipes at the same potential as the earth terminal
It prevents a voltage difference between two touchable surfaces
It stops electricity from flowing through a person touching two parts
teachChecks.hint: Protective devices stop the current; bonding conductors keep metal parts at the same voltage.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In the earthing system code TT, what does the French word 'Terre' translate to in English?
What component must an installer drive into the ground to provide an earth connection for a TT system?
In a TT system, where is the first 'T' (the source) connected?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the local earth electrode considered the most critical safety component in a TT installation compared to other systems?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The supplier does not provide an earth terminal
It is the only path for fault current to reach the ground
The fault current must travel through the soil to return to the source
Safety depends entirely on the installer's local connection
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), and the consumer must provide their own rod.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In the earthing system code TN-S, what does the letter 'S' stand for?
In a TN-C-S system, which conductor is the Earth combined with in the supplier's street cables?
What part of the supplier's cable is typically used as the separate earth path in a TN-S system?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: What is the primary difference in how the Neutral and Earth conductors are arranged as they enter a property in a TN-C-S system compared to a TN-S system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: In TN-C-S they arrive as one combined conductor
In TN-S they arrive as two separate conductors
TN-C-S splits the combined wire at the property entrance
TN-S maintains separation from the transformer to the property
teachChecks.hint: S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Earth and Neutral share a wire in the street.

teachChecks.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor

basicQuestions.questionText: Which conductor acts as the 'master' link between the Main Earthing Terminal and the means of earthing?
What is the specific name for the safety wire found inside a 'twin and earth' cable that protects a single circuit?
If a single CPC is disconnected, how much of the electrical installation loses its safety protection?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the disconnection of the Earthing Conductor considered a much more serious fault than the disconnection of a single CPC?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The Earthing Conductor protects the entire installation
Disconnection removes the safety path for every circuit
It is the single link to the actual means of earthing
A CPC failure only leaves one circuit unprotected
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is for one circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

While a fuse 'trips' to stop current, bonding 'links' metal parts to keep them at a safe, equal voltage."
      ],
      "hint": "Protective devices stop the current; bonding conductors keep metal parts at the same voltage."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (Terre is French for Earth). The first 'T' tells us that the source of the electricity (the transformer) is connected directly to Earth. The second 'T' tells us that the consumer's installation is also connected to Earth through its own local connection.",
        "In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-clad steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the building. Because the fault current must travel through the actual ground (soil) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is often higher than in other systems. This makes the local electrode the most critical component for safety in a TT installation, as it is the only path for fault current to reach the ground."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), and the consumer must provide their own rod."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the exposed metal parts are connected to the supplier's Neutral conductor. The final letter describes how the Earth and Neutral are arranged.",
        "In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer's property. Usually, the supplier's cable sheath acts as the separate earth path.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate.' In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into one single conductor (often called PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing). However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety."
      ],
      "hint": "S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Earth and Neutral share a wire in the street."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "While both are green and yellow wires, the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) and the Earthing Conductor have different roles based on their location in the system. A CPC is the safety wire for an individual circuit. For example, the green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a light switch or a socket is a CPC. Its job is to connect the metal casing of that specific equipment back to the consumer unit.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the 'master' earth wire for the entire installation. There is only one main Earthing Conductor in a standard building. It connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit to the actual means of earthing, such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod. If the Earthing Conductor is disconnected, the entire building loses its safety path. If a CPC is disconnected, only that one specific circuit loses its protection."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is for one circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house."
    }
  ]
}

output.rawText:
{
  "title": "Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and ADS Components",
  "objective": "Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a typical domestic installation scenario.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).",
    "Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).",
    "Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.",
    "Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.",
    "Step 4: Identify the Bonding Conductors. You notice two 10mm² green and yellow cables leaving the MET. One goes to the gas meter and one to the water stopcock. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors.",
    "Step 5: Identify the Protective Devices. Inside the consumer unit, you see a row of 6A and 32A Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs). These are the protective devices that will trip to provide the 'Disconnection' part of ADS.",
    "Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, Main Protective Bonding Conductors, and MCBs.",
    "Takeaway: Always trace the path from the supply to the circuit. The Earthing Conductor is the main link to the source, while CPCs are the safety wires for individual circuits. If the earth and neutral are joined at the cutout, it's TN-C-S."
  ],
  "hint": "Look closely at the supplier's cutout; if the earth wire originates from the neutral side of the block, you are looking at a TN-C-S (PME) system."
}

parsed.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and ADS Components
parsed.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a typical domestic installation scenario.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.

Step 4: Identify the Bonding Conductors. You notice two 10mm² green and yellow cables leaving the MET. One goes to the gas meter and one to the water stopcock. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors.

Step 5: Identify the Protective Devices. Inside the consumer unit, you see a row of 6A and 32A Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs). These are the protective devices that will trip to provide the 'Disconnection' part of ADS.
Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, Main Protective Bonding Conductors, and MCBs.

Takeaway: Always trace the path from the supply to the circuit. The Earthing Conductor is the main link to the source, while CPCs are the safety wires for individual circuits. If the earth and neutral are joined at the cutout, it's TN-C-S.
parsed.hint: Look closely at the supplier's cutout; if the earth wire originates from the neutral side of the block, you are looking at a TN-C-S (PME) system.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

    "Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.",
    "Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.",
    "Step 4: Identify the Bonding Conductors. You notice two 10mm² green and yellow cables leaving the MET. One goes to the gas meter and one to the water stopcock. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors.",
    "Step 5: Identify the Protective Devices. Inside the consumer unit, you see a row of 6A and 32A Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs). These are the protective devices that will trip to provide the 'Disconnection' part of ADS.",
    "Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, Main Protective Bonding Conductors, and MCBs.",
    "Takeaway: Always trace the path from the supply to the circuit. The Earthing Conductor is the main link to the source, while CPCs are the safety wires for individual circuits. If the earth and neutral are joined at the cutout, it's TN-C-S."
  ],
  "hint": "Look closely at the supplier's cutout; if the earth wire originates from the neutral side of the block, you are looking at a TN-C-S (PME) system."
}

      "Observation 1: There is a metal clamp on the lead sheath of the incoming cable.",
      "Observation 2: A 16mm² green and yellow cable runs from this clamp to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).",
      "Observation 3: The neutral and earth paths are separate all the way back to the substation.",
      "Recall: If the earth is provided by the cable sheath and is kept separate from the neutral, it is a 'Separate' system."
    ],
    "questionText": "Based on the scenario, identify the specific earthing system type and the name of the 16mm² conductor that connects the lead sheath clamp to the MET.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "TN-S and Earthing Conductor",
      "TN-S system and Earthing Conductor",
      "TNS and Earthing Conductor"
    ],

Grounded source text:
Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Excerpt 1 (d9a6caef-6a5f-4977-9780-209ce55a5692): ## LO4 — Requirements for earthing systems **AC** 4.1 Identify earthing system types 4.2 Identify ADS component parts 4.3 Identify exposed conductive parts 4.4 Identify extraneous conductive parts 4.5 Identify components of an earth loop impedance path **Range** - Earthing systems: TT; TN-S; TN-C-S - ADS components: CPC; main protective bonding; supplementary bonding; earthing conductor; protective devices; earth electrode - Exposed conductive parts: steel conduit/trunking/tray; steel enclosures; metal accessories; metallic equipment - Extraneous conductive parts: metallic gas/oil/water pipes; steel ductwork; structural steel - Earth loop impedance path components: Zs; Ze; R1; R2; MET; supplier earth return path ---

{

    {
      "term": "ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply)",
      "simpleDefinition": "A safety strategy where a fault causes a protective device to automatically cut off power to prevent electric shock.",
      "anchor": "The coordinated system of earthing and fuses working together to kill the power during a fault.",

      "misconception": "Calling every green and yellow wire an 'Earthing Conductor' when most are actually CPCs or bonding."

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "ADS Component Identification",
      "objective": "Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single piece of equipment, but a coordinated safety system designed to prevent electric shock. For ADS to work, it requires two main things: a low-resistance path for fault current to flow, and a protective device to sense that current and cut the power.",
        "The system is made up of several specific components. These include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) which runs to each individual outlet, and the Earthing Conductor which connects the whole building to the ground. It also includes Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding, which connect metal pipes to the earth terminal. Finally, the system relies on Protective Devices, such as fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), to perform the actual 'disconnection' when a fault occurs. In some installations, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the soil) is also required to provide the connection to the earth itself."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team of parts working together to provide a path and a switch."
    },
    {
      "title": "Bonding and Protective Devices",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In an ADS system, different components have very different jobs. Protective devices, such as fuses and circuit breakers, are the 'switches' of the system. Their only job is to detect a high current during a fault and physically break the circuit to stop the flow of electricity.",
        "Bonding conductors do not break the circuit; instead, they ensure safety by equalizing voltage. Main Protective Bonding connects incoming metal services, like gas and water mains, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs, these pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth, preventing a person from getting a shock by touching a pipe and an appliance at the same time. Supplementary Bonding is used in specific high-risk areas, like bathrooms, to connect metal parts together locally. While a fuse 'trips' to stop current, bonding 'links' metal parts to keep them at a safe, equal voltage."
      ],
      "hint": "Protective devices stop the current; bonding conductors keep metal parts at the same voltage."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (Terre is French for Earth). The first 'T' tells us that the source of the electricity (the transformer) is connected directly to Earth. The second 'T' tells us that the consumer's installation is also connected to Earth through its own local connection.",
        "In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-clad steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the building. Because the fault current must travel through the actual ground (soil) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is often higher than in other systems. This makes the local electrode the most critical component for safety in a TT installation, as it is the only path for fault current to reach the ground."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), and the consumer must provide their own rod."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the exposed metal parts are connected to the supplier's Neutral conductor. The final letter describes how the Earth and Neutral are arranged.",
        "In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer's property. Usually, the supplier's cable sheath acts as the separate earth path.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate.' In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into one single conductor (often called PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing). However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety."
      ],
      "hint": "S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Earth and Neutral share a wire in the street."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "While both are green and yellow wires, the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) and the Earthing Conductor have different roles based on their location in the system. A CPC is the safety wire for an individual circuit. For example, the green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a light switch or a socket is a CPC. Its job is to connect the metal casing of that specific equipment back to the consumer unit.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the 'master' earth wire for the entire installation. There is only one main Earthing Conductor in a standard building. It connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit to the actual means of earthing, such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod. If the Earthing Conductor is disconnected, the entire building loses its safety path. If a CPC is disconnected, only that one specific circuit loses its protection."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is for one circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house."
    }
  ]
}

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

steps.title: ADS Component Identification
steps.objective: Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.
keyIdeas: The list of components including CPC, bonding conductors, earthing conductor, and protective devices.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installing or testing them.

Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single piece of equipment, but a coordinated safety system designed to prevent electric shock. For ADS to work, it requires two main things: a low-resistance path for fault current to flow, and a protective device to sense that current and cut the power.

The system is made up of several specific components. These include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) which runs to each individual outlet, and the Earthing Conductor which connects the whole building to the ground. It also includes Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding, which connect metal pipes to the earth terminal. Finally, the system relies on Protective Devices, such as fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), to perform the actual 'disconnection' when a fault occurs. In some installations, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the soil) is also required to provide the connection to the earth itself.
Explanation A

In some installations, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the soil) is also required to provide the connection to the earth itself.

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is a low-resistance path just as essential as a circuit breaker for the ADS system to function correctly?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installing or testing them.
deeperAnswerGuidance: The path allows fault current to flow back to the source; High current is needed to trigger the protective device; Without a path the device will not detect the fault; It ensures the disconnection happens fast enough to prevent shock

steps.title: Bonding and Protective Devices
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices.
keyIdeas: The specific roles of bonding conductors versus the role of circuit breakers or fuses in ADS.
Incorrectly identifying bonding types leads to non-compliance with BS 7671 and safety risks.

In an ADS system, different components have very different jobs. Protective devices, such as fuses and circuit breakers, are the 'switches' of the system. Their only job is to detect a high current during a fault and physically break the circuit to stop the flow of electricity.

Bonding conductors do not break the circuit; instead, they ensure safety by equalizing voltage. Main Protective Bonding connects incoming metal services, like gas and water mains, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs, these pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth, preventing a person from getting a shock by touching a pipe and an appliance at the same time. Supplementary Bonding is used in specific high-risk areas, like bathrooms, to connect metal parts together locally. While a fuse 'trips' to stop current, bonding 'links' metal parts to keep them at a safe, equal voltage.
Explanation B

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: The TT arrangement where the consumer provides their own connection to Earth via an electrode.
TT systems rely on the soil for the fault path, making the electrode critical for safety.

Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (Terre is French for Earth). The first 'T' tells us that the source of the electricity (the transformer) is connected directly to Earth. The second 'T' tells us that the consumer's installation is also connected to Earth through its own local connection.

In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-clad steel rod driven deep into the soil outside the building. Because the fault current must travel through the actual ground (soil) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is often higher than in other systems. This makes the local electrode the most critical component for safety in a TT installation, as it is the only path for fault current to reach the ground.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
keyIdeas: The difference between separate (S) and combined (C) neutral and earth conductors in the supply.
The type of TN system dictates how the MET is connected to the supplier's equipment.

TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the exposed metal parts are connected to the supplier's Neutral conductor. The final letter describes how the Earth and Neutral are arranged.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer's property. Usually, the supplier's cable sheath acts as the separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate.' In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into one single conductor (often called PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing). However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety.
Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely separate wires all the way from the transformer to the consumer's property. Usually, the supplier's cable sheath acts as the separate earth path.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate.' In the supplier's street cables, the Neutral and Earth are 'Combined' into one single conductor (often called PME, or Protective Multiple Earthing). However, when the cable enters the property, this single wire is split into 'Separate' Neutral and Earth terminals. TN-C-S is the most common modern system, but it relies on that one combined wire remaining intact for safety.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking TN-S and TN-C-S are interchangeable in terms of their internal wiring requirements.
S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Earth and Neutral share a wire in the street.

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is TN-C-S also described as a system where neutral and earth are combined first and then separated?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: The type of TN system dictates how the MET is connected to the supplier's equipment.

steps.hint: S = Separate, C = Combined, PEN = Protective Earth and Neutral.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the means of earthing; the CPC connects exposed conductive parts to the MET.
Using the wrong term during inspection and testing results in incorrect documentation and confusion.

While both are green and yellow wires, the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) and the Earthing Conductor have different roles based on their location in the system. A CPC is the safety wire for an individual circuit. For example, the green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that goes to a light switch or a socket is a CPC. Its job is to connect the metal casing of that specific equipment back to the consumer unit.

The Earthing Conductor is the 'master' earth wire for the entire installation. There is only one main Earthing Conductor in a standard building. It connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit to the actual means of earthing, such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod. If the Earthing Conductor is disconnected, the entire building loses its safety path. If a CPC is disconnected, only that one specific circuit loses its protection.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and ADS Components
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a typical domestic installation scenario.
keyIdeas: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a typical domestic installation scenario.
Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.

Step 4: Identify the Bonding Conductors. You notice two 10mm² green and yellow cables leaving the MET. One goes to the gas meter and one to the water stopcock. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are inspecting a 1990s semi-detached house. At the intake position, you see a single incoming concentric cable. Inside the supplier's cutout (the big fuse before the meter), the earth terminal is connected directly to the neutral block. From there, a 16mm² green and yellow cable runs to a Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
steps: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cutout, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).
Identify the Earthing Conductor. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting the supplier's terminal to the MET is the Earthing Conductor. It is the master link for the whole house.

Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). You open the consumer unit and see smaller green and yellow wires (1.5mm² and 2.5mm²) connected to the earth bar. These are the CPCs for the lighting and socket circuits.

Identify the Bonding Conductors. You notice two 10mm² green and yellow cables leaving the MET. One goes to the gas meter and one to the water stopcock. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors.

Identify the Protective Devices. Inside the consumer unit, you see a row of 6A and 32A Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs). These are the protective devices that will trip to provide the 'Disconnection' part of ADS.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Always trace the path from the supply to the circuit. The Earthing Conductor is the main link to the source, while CPCs are the safety wires for individual circuits. If the earth and neutral are joined at the cutout, it's TN-C-S.

steps.hint: Look closely at the supplier's cutout; if the earth wire originates from the neutral side of the block, you are looking at a TN-C-S (PME) system.

steps.hint: The key issue is not the label TT by itself; it is the resistance of the earth return path.

spacedReview.title: Prerequisite: Circuit Protection Fundamentals
spacedReview.objective: Recall the fundamental purpose of circuit protection and the concept of a fault current path before identifying specific earthing arrangements.
retrievalTextLines: A 'fault' occurs when electricity takes an unintended path, often to the metallic casing of an appliance.
For a protective device (like a fuse or MCB) to trip instantly, there must be a clear, low-resistance path back to the source.
If this path is missing or high-resistance, the metal casing remains live, creating a lethal shock risk.
The 'Automatic' part of ADS relies entirely on this path being correctly installed and maintained.
spacedReview.questionText: In the event of an earth fault, what is the specific job of the protective device (fuse or MCB), and what does it require to do that job effectively?
answerGuidance: The device must automatically disconnect the supply (cut the power) to prevent shock or fire.
It requires a low-resistance path (the earth path) to allow enough fault current to flow to trigger the device.
Without a complete path back to the transformer, the device will not 'see' the fault and will not trip.

Spaced review artifact:
{
  "title": "Prerequisite: Circuit Protection Fundamentals",
  "objective": "Recall the fundamental purpose of circuit protection and the concept of a fault current path before identifying specific earthing arrangements.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "A 'fault' occurs when electricity takes an unintended path, often to the metallic casing of an appliance.",
    "For a protective device (like a fuse or MCB) to trip instantly, there must be a clear, low-resistance path back to the source.",
    "If this path is missing or high-resistance, the metal casing remains live, creating a lethal shock risk.",
    "The 'Automatic' part of ADS relies entirely on this path being correctly installed and maintained."
  ],
  "questionText": "In the event of an earth fault, what is the specific job of the protective device (fuse or MCB), and what does it require to do that job effectively?",
  "answerGuidance": [
    "The device must automatically disconnect the supply (cut the power) to prevent shock or fire.",
    "It requires a low-resistance path (the earth path) to allow enough fault current to flow to trigger the device.",
    "Without a complete path back to the transformer, the device will not 'see' the fault and will not trip."
  ]
}

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
